module: Immunocompromised patients Part III: Managing the Immunocompromised Patient

sections.The neutropenic patient: Severe neutropenia: ANC <500 cells/µL (or expected to fall to <500 within 48 h). Agranulocytosis: ANC <200 cells/µL. Fever definition: single oral temperature >38.3°C OR ≥38.0°C sustained over one hour. Frequency and severity of infection is inversely proportional to neutrophil count; risks are particularly high below <200 cells/µL. Approximately 80% of neutropenic febrile patients are 'considered infected' but a causative organism is identified in fewer than 50%; bacteraemia documented in only 10–25%. Gram-positive bacteria now predominate (S. epidermidis most common), though gram-negatives cause more severe infections. High-risk criteria (ANY of): MASCC score <21; CISNE score ≥3 (solid tumours); anticipated severe neutropenia >7 days; alemtuzumab or CAR-T within past 2 months; sepsis/septic shock; mucositis; GI symptoms; intravascular catheter infection; new pulmonary infiltrate or hypoxaemia; chronic lung disease; hepatic or renal insufficiency; uncontrolled malignancy. Antibiotics must be started within 60 minutes after blood cultures. CT preferred over plain radiographs for pulmonary infiltrates and neutropenic enterocolitis. Empiric monotherapy: anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or carbapenem). Vancomycin NOT routine — add only for: haemodynamic instability; pneumonia; known MRSA colonisation; gram-positive bacteraemia pending speciation; suspected catheter or skin/soft tissue infection. Persistent fever 4–7 days despite antibacterials with expected neutropenia >7 days: add empirical antifungal (liposomal amphotericin B or echinocandin). In patients on fluconazole prophylaxis: breakthrough infections from fluconazole-resistant Candida (C. glabrata, C. krusei) or Aspergillus. Low-risk (MASCC ≥21): oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate; early discharge feasible. CVC removal mandated for CRBSI with S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, Candida spp., non-tuberculous mycobacteria. Colonoscopy is contraindicated in neutropenic enterocolitis (perforation risk). Routine G-CSF for established febrile neutropenia is not recommended.
sections.Managing respiratory failure in the neut: Respiratory failure is common after chemotherapy and HSCT. Infectious causes in approximately 50% of cases. CT scan is preferred over plain CXR in neutropenic patients. Galactomannan testing in blood and BAL recommended for Aspergillus diagnosis; BAL sensitivity is higher than serum. CT signs of angioinvasive mould: pulmonary nodules, halo sign, vessel occlusion sign. NIV and HFOT data are conflicting; intubation should NOT be delayed if non-invasive support fails.
sections.The ICU patient with HIV disease: PCP is the most common AIDS-defining cause of ICU admission. PCP in HIV: gradual onset over 2–4 weeks (contrast: fulminant in transplant/haematological patients); CD4 <200 cells/µL; HRCT bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities sparing periphery with intra-parenchymal cysts. Treatment: TMP-SMX plus prednisone when PaO2 <70 mmHg on room air. PCP does NOT cause septic shock — search for bacterial superinfection if shock present. In-hospital HIV mortality depends on organ dysfunction and comorbidities, NOT on CD4, viral load, or prior cART. IRIS: paradoxical worsening of treated/undiagnosed opportunistic infection weeks-months after cART; do not interrupt cART except in life-threatening cases; treat with prednisone (not if cryptococcal meningitis or Kaposi's). CD4 thresholds — <200: PCP, miliary/extrapulmonary TB; <150: Kaposi's sarcoma (HHV-8), lymphoma; <100: Candida oesophagitis, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis; <50: MAC, disseminated CMV.
sections.Infection following solid organ transpla: Three infection periods after SOT. First month: donor-derived or pre-existing recipient infections + surgical complications (Candida, aerobic gram-negatives, gram-positives, anastomotic infections, catheter-related, UTI). HSV reactivation largely prevented by prophylactic aciclovir. Months 1–6: greatest risk for opportunistic infections — CMV, PCP (TMP-SMX prophylaxis), Cryptococcus, Aspergillus, EBV, VZV, HSV, HHV-6/7/8, toxoplasmosis, community respiratory viruses. CMV risk: D+/R- = highest; D+/R+ and D-/R+ = intermediate; D-/R- = lowest. Ganciclovir/valganciclovir prophylaxis: effective but myelosuppressive (neutropenia common complication). CMV diagnosis: quantitative PCR or pp65 antigenemia assay; serology has NO diagnostic value for active disease. CMV end-organ manifestations: pneumonitis, hepatitis, oesophagitis, colitis, retinitis, encephalitis. CMV treatment: ganciclovir (first-line); ganciclovir-resistant CMV: foscarnet or cidofovir (high toxicity). >6 months: community-acquired pathogens predominate; those with chronic rejection requiring high immunosuppression remain at elevated risk. HBV: entecavir or tenofovir pretransplant; detectable HBV DNA at transplant = main reinfection risk factor; antiviral therapy continued indefinitely post-transplant. HCV (EASL): Child-Pugh B/C, MELD <18–20 → treat with DAA pretransplant; MELD >18–20 → transplant first, treat post-transplant (if anticipated wait <6 months, otherwise treat pretransplant). HCV reinfection without antiviral therapy is universal.
sections.Infection following haematopoietic stem: Three HSCT infection phases. Pre-engraftment (day 0–30): profound neutropenia; bacterial infections and Candida predominate; HSV reactivation common. Early post-engraftment (day 30–100): CMV (up to 80% reactivation in allogeneic without prophylaxis), PCP (TMP-SMX prophylaxis), Aspergillus, GVHD-related complications; diarrhoea most commonly from acute GVHD (also consider CMV colitis, C. difficile). Late post-engraftment (>day 100): low risk unless chronic GVHD; chronic GVHD → continued immunosuppression → high infection risk; encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis) from functional asplenia; Aspergillus; PCP (if prophylaxis stopped); VZV. Adenovirus: reactivation from GI tract; high DNA load = poor prognosis; disseminated disease mortality 8–26%; risk factors: T-cell depletion, myeloablative conditioning, severe GVHD, mismatched donor; cidofovir most widely used antiviral. CMV strategy: prophylactic ganciclovir/valganciclovir (myelosuppressive) OR pre-emptive therapy (regular PCR/antigenemia screening).

questions.question: A 58-year-old man with AML is receiving induction chemotherapy. He is afebrile on morning ward rounds but develops an oral temperature of 38.5°C at 14:00. His ANC from this morning is 380 cells/µL. He has a tunnelled central venous catheter and is haemodynamically stable with no localising symptoms. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: A: Draw blood cultures and begin broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics within 60 minutes
B: Repeat the temperature measurement in 1–2 hours before initiating antibiotics
C: Start oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate and observe as an outpatient
D: Perform CT chest and abdomen to identify a source before antibiotic initiation
E: Administer intravenous fluconazole as fungi are the most common early cause of neutropenic fever
questions.explanation: Any neutropenic patient with fever is a medical emergency requiring blood cultures followed by broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 60 minutes. A single temperature >38.3°C is sufficient — waiting for repeat measurement (option B) delays treatment and increases mortality risk. Option C is the correct approach only for low-risk febrile neutropenia (MASCC ≥21, solid tumour, no comorbidities), which does not apply here. Fungi rarely cause the first febrile episode; bacteria (predominantly gram-positive) are the primary early pathogens — so fluconazole alone is inappropriate.
questions.topic: febrile neutropenia emergency blood cultures antibiotics 60 minutes AML ANC

questions.question: A 52-year-old woman with breast cancer receiving adjuvant chemotherapy develops fever (38.5°C). Her ANC is 620 cells/µL, expected to nadir at 450 cells/µL for fewer than 5 days. She has no haemodynamic instability, no mucositis, no indwelling catheter, no intra-abdominal symptoms, and no significant comorbidities. Her MASCC score is 24. Which management strategy is most appropriate?
options: A: Admit and commence IV piperacillin-tazobactam
B: Prescribe oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate; early hospital discharge is appropriate
C: Commence IV cefepime plus vancomycin
D: Administer IV meropenem to cover possible ESBL-producing organisms
E: Start oral fluconazole prophylaxis only and monitor as outpatient without antibacterials
questions.explanation: A MASCC score ≥21 identifies low-risk febrile neutropenia. For low-risk patients with solid tumours and no high-risk features, oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate with early or ambulatory management is guideline-recommended. Option A uses an appropriate antibiotic but involves unnecessary inpatient IV therapy for a low-risk patient. Option C adds vancomycin without an indication. Meropenem is reserved for ESBL-suspected or treatment-failure scenarios, not first-line empiric therapy for low-risk patients. Antibacterials cannot be omitted in febrile neutropenia.
questions.topic: MASCC ≥21 low-risk febrile neutropenia oral outpatient ciprofloxacin amoxicillin-clavulanate

questions.question: A 61-year-old man with ALL is admitted with high-risk febrile neutropenia (ANC 210 cells/µL, expected duration >7 days, MASCC score 18). He is haemodynamically stable with no localising signs, no drug allergies, and no prior colonisation with resistant organisms. Which is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic approach?
options: A: IV piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy
B: IV vancomycin monotherapy
C: IV tobramycin (aminoglycoside) monotherapy
D: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy
E: Oral ciprofloxacin monotherapy
questions.explanation: Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy — piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or a carbapenem — is the standard empiric regimen for high-risk febrile neutropenia. Vancomycin alone provides no gram-negative cover and is not a recommended monotherapy. Aminoglycoside monotherapy (option C) is not recommended as sole initial therapy due to inferior efficacy and significant nephrotoxicity in this setting. Ceftriaxone lacks reliable anti-pseudomonal activity. Oral therapy is inappropriate for high-risk febrile neutropenia.
questions.topic: high-risk febrile neutropenia empiric monotherapy pip-tazo cefepime carbapenem anti-pseudomonal

questions.question: A 55-year-old patient with lymphoma and high-risk febrile neutropenia has just been commenced on IV piperacillin-tazobactam. The team discusses whether to add vancomycin. Which of the following is NOT an indication to add a glycopeptide to the empiric regimen?
options: A: Haemodynamic instability or signs of severe sepsis
B: Suspected tunnelled catheter-related bloodstream infection
C: Fever persisting for 24 hours despite adequate beta-lactam therapy
D: Known colonisation with MRSA
E: Positive blood culture for gram-positive organisms while awaiting speciation
questions.explanation: Vancomycin is not recommended as a routine addition to empiric febrile neutropenia regimens. Validated indications include: haemodynamic instability or severe sepsis; pneumonia; known MRSA colonisation; gram-positive bacteraemia pending speciation; and suspected catheter, skin or soft tissue infection. Persistence of fever for 24 hours alone is NOT an indication for vancomycin — it is simply expected in many patients early in therapy. Fever persisting for 4–7 days despite adequate antibacterials, in the context of prolonged expected neutropenia, is the trigger for empirical antifungal therapy, not vancomycin.
questions.topic: vancomycin NOT indication neutropenic fever 24h persistence antifungal persistent fever trigger

questions.question: A 48-year-old woman with AML has been receiving IV piperacillin-tazobactam for 6 days for high-risk febrile neutropenia. She remains febrile (38.4°C), ANC is 90 cells/µL expected to remain <500 cells/µL for at least 5 more days. Blood cultures are persistently negative and there are no new clinical findings. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Broaden to IV meropenem to cover possible ESBL-producing organisms
B: Add IV vancomycin for possible occult gram-positive bacteraemia
C: Add empirical antifungal therapy (echinocandin or liposomal amphotericin B)
D: Commence granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF)
E: Discontinue antibacterials and restart after obtaining new cultures
questions.explanation: Persistent fever for 4–7 days despite broad-spectrum antibacterials in a patient with expected prolonged neutropenia (>7 days) is the principal indication for empirical antifungal therapy targeting Candida and Aspergillus. Option A is reasonable if gram-negative resistance is clinically suspected but does not address the unmet need for antifungal cover. Vancomycin is not indicated for uncomplicated fever persistence. Routine G-CSF is not recommended for established febrile neutropenia. Stopping antibacterials without replacement in ongoing neutropenia and fever would be dangerous.
questions.topic: persistent neutropenic fever 4-7 days empiric antifungal echinocandin liposomal amphotericin

questions.question: A 40-year-old man with AML and ANC 70 cells/µL develops fever, crampy abdominal pain, and bloody diarrhoea. CT abdomen shows diffuse bowel wall thickening in the right colon. Neutropenic enterocolitis is suspected. Which of the following should be AVOIDED?
options: A: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
B: Blood cultures including simultaneous peripheral vein and CVC sets
C: Colonoscopy to obtain tissue for microbiological diagnosis
D: Empirical antibiotics including anaerobic coverage
E: Stool culture and Clostridioides difficile toxin testing
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is contraindicated in neutropenic enterocolitis because air insufflation and instrument manipulation carry a high risk of bowel perforation through the inflamed and friable wall. CT scan is the diagnostic method of choice, identifying bowel wall thickening that may not be apparent on plain films. Blood cultures, stool cultures and C. difficile testing are all appropriate investigations. Antibiotics including anaerobic coverage are a cornerstone of management. Surgical intervention is reserved for confirmed perforation.
questions.topic: neutropenic enterocolitis colonoscopy contraindicated perforation CT blood cultures antibiotics anaerobic

questions.question: A 63-year-old man with multiple myeloma has a tunnelled CVC. Blood cultures drawn simultaneously from the CVC and a peripheral vein both grow the same organism, confirming a catheter-related bloodstream infection. For which of the following organisms is immediate CVC removal mandated over antibiotic lock therapy?
options: A: Coagulase-negative Staphylococcus
B: Enterococcus faecalis
C: Candida parapsilosis
D: Streptococcus viridans
E: Corynebacterium jeikeium
questions.explanation: CVC removal combined with antifungal therapy is mandated for catheter-related bloodstream infections caused by Candida species (as well as S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and non-tuberculous mycobacteria) due to the risk of disseminated candidiasis, endophthalmitis, and endocarditis with retained hardware. Coagulase-negative Staphylococcus is the near-miss — it may be managed with antibiotic lock therapy if catheter preservation is strongly desired — but Candida is an absolute indication for removal. Enterococcus, Streptococcus viridans, and Corynebacterium generally permit a catheter-preservation approach with lock therapy.
questions.topic: CVC removal mandated Candida S aureus Pseudomonas lock therapy coagulase-negative permitted

questions.question: A CMV-seronegative patient (R−) is scheduled to receive a kidney from a CMV-seropositive donor (D+). Which statement best characterises this donor–recipient combination with respect to CMV infection risk after transplantation?
options: A: This represents the lowest risk combination for CMV infection
B: This represents the highest risk combination for CMV infection
C: Risk is intermediate, equivalent to the D+/R+ combination
D: Risk is equivalent to the D−/R+ combination as prior serostatus of the recipient is irrelevant
E: Routine CMV surveillance is not required as primary infection is rare post-transplant
questions.explanation: The D+/R− combination carries the highest CMV risk because the seronegative recipient has no pre-existing CMV-specific T-cell or antibody immunity and is exposed to donor-derived viral load under iatrogenic immunosuppression. The D+/R+ combination (option C, the near-miss) also carries high risk from potential superinfection with donor virus, but residual host immunity provides partial protection, making it intermediate rather than the highest risk. D−/R+ is intermediate and D−/R− is the lowest risk. Anti-CMV prophylaxis or pre-emptive monitoring is standard for D+/R− recipients.
questions.topic: CMV D+R- highest risk seronegative recipient seropositive donor SOT risk stratification

questions.question: A 67-year-old liver transplant recipient presents 8 weeks post-transplant with 10 days of fever, fatigue, leukopenia, elevated transaminases, and a dry cough. Active CMV disease is suspected. Which of the following investigations is LEAST useful for confirming active CMV disease?
options: A: Quantitative CMV PCR in blood
B: CMV pp65 antigenemia assay
C: Bronchoalveolar lavage for CMV culture and PCR
D: CMV IgM and IgG serology
E: Histological examination of a liver biopsy specimen for CMV inclusion bodies
questions.explanation: CMV serology (IgM/IgG) has no diagnostic value for active CMV disease in transplant recipients. Under immunosuppression, antibody responses are unreliable markers of active viral replication; the pre-transplant serological status is used for risk stratification only. Quantitative PCR and pp65 antigenemia are the validated methods to detect and quantify active viraemia. BAL with CMV PCR and culture is valuable for diagnosing CMV pneumonitis. Liver biopsy showing cytomegalic inclusion bodies is diagnostic of CMV hepatitis as end-organ disease.
questions.topic: CMV serology NOT useful active disease PCR antigenemia BAL histology diagnosis

questions.question: A kidney transplant recipient on tacrolimus and mycophenolate at 3 months post-transplant develops CMV pneumonitis with quantitative PCR viral load 45,000 IU/mL. What is the first-line treatment?
options: A: Foscarnet IV
B: Cidofovir IV
C: Ganciclovir IV
D: Aciclovir IV
E: High-dose oral valganciclovir
questions.explanation: Ganciclovir is the established treatment of choice for CMV disease in immunocompromised patients. IV ganciclovir is preferred over oral valganciclovir for serious manifestations such as pneumonitis, where reliable bioavailability of the oral agent cannot be guaranteed. Aciclovir has minimal activity against CMV. Foscarnet and cidofovir (options A and B) are reserved for ganciclovir-resistant CMV but carry substantial nephrotoxicity. The near-miss is option E: valganciclovir is used for prophylaxis and pre-emptive therapy of mild or moderate disease, but IV ganciclovir is the preferred first-line agent for end-organ CMV disease.
questions.topic: CMV pneumonitis treatment ganciclovir IV first-line valganciclovir oral prophylaxis foscarnet resistance

questions.question: A 47-year-old man is readmitted to ICU on day 26 after renal transplantation with fever and a surgical wound infection. Wound cultures yield Candida albicans. Which of the following best explains the timing and type of infection?
options: A: An opportunistic infection typical of the 1–6 month post-transplant period due to cumulative immunosuppression
B: A donor-derived or surgical site complication characteristic of the first month post-transplant
C: A late community-acquired infection reflecting residual immunosuppression
D: This likely represents colonisation only; antifungal therapy should be deferred pending repeat cultures
E: A manifestation of CMV-induced immunosuppression enabling secondary fungal breakthrough
questions.explanation: In the first month after solid organ transplantation, infections are dominated by donor-derived pathogens and surgical complications — including anastomotic infections, haematoma superinfection, surgical site infections, catheter-related infections and UTIs. Candida species frequently colonise both recipient and donor pretransplant and can emerge as surgical site pathogens in this early window. The 1–6 month period (option A, the near-miss) represents peak risk for opportunistic infections such as CMV and PCP, not surgical site candidiasis 26 days post-operatively.
questions.topic: SOT first month surgical donor-derived Candida wound infection timeline not opportunistic

questions.question: A 34-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV (no prior cART) is admitted to ICU with a 3-week history of progressive dyspnoea and non-productive cough. CD4 count is 145 cells/µL. PaO2 on room air is 62 mmHg. High-resolution CT shows bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities sparing the periphery with intraparenchymal cysts. Which treatment regimen is most appropriate?
options: A: IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
B: TMP-SMX plus prednisone
C: TMP-SMX alone
D: IV ganciclovir
E: IV liposomal amphotericin B
questions.explanation: This presentation is classic for PCP: subacute course over weeks, CD4 <200 cells/µL, perihilar bilateral ground-glass opacities with peripheral sparing and intraparenchymal cysts. TMP-SMX is the treatment of choice. Adjunctive prednisone is mandatory when PaO2 <70 mmHg on room air, reducing respiratory deterioration and mortality by attenuating the inflammatory response to dying fungi. Option C is the near-miss — TMP-SMX is correct but omits prednisone, which is critically important at this level of hypoxaemia. CMV pneumonitis (option D) is much less likely with this subacute presentation and typical CT pattern.
questions.section: The ICU patient with HIV disease
questions.topic: HIV PCP TMP-SMX prednisone PaO2 <70 mmHg CD4 <200 perihilar ground-glass cysts

questions.question: A 29-year-old woman with HIV/AIDS is diagnosed with PCP (PaO2 76 mmHg on room air) and starts TMP-SMX. cART is initiated on day 5 of PCP treatment. Two weeks after starting cART she develops worsening hypoxaemia and new bilateral infiltrates despite ongoing PCP treatment. Repeat BAL finds no new pathogen. What is the most likely explanation?
options: A: Secondary bacterial pneumonia superimposed on PCP
B: TMP-SMX adverse reaction causing pneumonitis
C: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)
D: PCP treatment failure requiring escalation to IV pentamidine
E: CMV pneumonitis emerging after immune reconstitution
questions.explanation: IRIS is a paradoxical worsening of a treated or unrecognised opportunistic infection occurring weeks to months after cART initiation, as recovering immune function mounts an exaggerated inflammatory response to residual antigens. Respiratory deterioration in a PCP patient who has started cART, in the absence of a new pathogen on repeat BAL, is the canonical scenario. cART should not be interrupted except in life-threatening cases; treatment with prednisone is appropriate (unless the infection is cryptococcal meningitis or Kaposi's sarcoma). The near-miss is option D — treatment failure is possible but requires microbiological confirmation; the temporal link to cART and negative BAL strongly favours IRIS.
questions.section: The ICU patient with HIV disease
questions.topic: IRIS cART initiation PCP paradoxical worsening immune reconstitution do not stop cART prednisone

questions.question: A 14-year-old boy underwent T-cell-depleted allogeneic HSCT 45 days ago (myeloablative conditioning, unrelated mismatched donor). He now presents with high adenovirus DNA load in blood, along with pneumonia, hepatitis, haemorrhagic cystitis and colitis. Which antiviral agent is most widely used for treating disseminated adenovirus disease in this setting?
options: A: Ganciclovir
B: Aciclovir
C: Cidofovir
D: Foscarnet
E: Ribavirin
questions.explanation: Cidofovir is the most widely used antiviral for adenovirus infection in HSCT patients. The clinical picture — post-allogeneic HSCT with T-cell depletion, myeloablative conditioning, mismatched unrelated donor, day 45 — represents the highest-risk profile for adenovirus reactivation from the GI tract. Disseminated adenovirus carries a mortality of 8–26%, demanding prompt treatment. Ganciclovir (the near-miss, given the post-engraftment timing suggesting CMV risk) has no meaningful activity against adenovirus. Aciclovir and foscarnet are antiherpesvirals. Treatment also includes reducing immunosuppressive therapy where possible.
questions.section: Infection following haematopoietic stem cell transplantation
questions.topic: adenovirus HSCT cidofovir allogeneic T-cell depletion disseminated 8-26% mortality GI reactivation

questions.question: A 55-year-old woman with HCV-related decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B, MELD score 14) without hepatocellular carcinoma is listed for liver transplantation. According to EASL guidance, what is the most appropriate approach regarding direct-acting antiviral (DAA) therapy?
options: A: Defer all HCV treatment until after liver transplantation
B: Initiate DAA therapy now, before transplantation
C: Wait until MELD exceeds 18–20 before starting DAA therapy pretransplant
D: Treat only if HCV RNA is spontaneously undetectable on serial testing
E: Proceed directly to transplantation regardless of MELD because post-transplant DAA is equally effective
questions.explanation: EASL recommends that patients with decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B or C) without HCC awaiting liver transplantation should receive DAA therapy when their MELD score is <18–20. This patient's MELD of 14 falls below this threshold, making pretransplant treatment the appropriate choice — HCV eradication prevents the otherwise universal graft reinfection and associated rapid progressive disease. The near-miss is option C, which inverts the EASL criterion: MELD >18–20 patients lack sufficient hepatic reserve and should be transplanted first, then treated post-transplant. Without any antiviral therapy, HCV reinfection of the new graft is universal.
questions.topic: HCV liver transplant EASL DAA pretransplant MELD <18-20 Child-Pugh decompensated cirrhosis

questions.question: Regarding definitions and epidemiology of febrile neutropenia:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Severe neutropenia is defined as ANC <500 cells/µL (or expected to fall below 500 within 48 hours).

statements.label: B
statements.text: Neutropenic fever is defined as a single oral temperature >38.3°C OR ≥38.0°C sustained over one hour.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A causative organism is identified in more than 50% of febrile neutropenic episodes.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Gram-positive bacteria now account for the majority of documented bacteraemias in neutropenic patients.

questions.explanation: Severe neutropenia is ANC <500 cells/µL (or predicted to reach this within 48 h); ANC <200 cells/µL defines agranulocytosis. The fever definition requires either a single temperature >38.3°C or sustained ≥38.0°C for one hour. Statement C is false: approximately 80% of patients are 'considered infected' but a causative organism is identified in fewer than 50%, and bacteraemia is documented in only 10–25% of episodes. The shift to gram-positive predominance reflects increased CVC use, broad-spectrum prophylaxis against gram-negatives, and newer chemotherapy regimens — though gram-negatives remain associated with more severe sepsis.
questions.topic: neutropenia severe ANC 500 fever 38.3 causative organism <50% gram-positive predominance bacteraemia

questions.question: Regarding risk stratification in patients with febrile neutropenia:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A MASCC score <21 identifies patients at high risk for serious complications.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A CISNE score ≥3 identifies high-risk patients among those with solid tumours.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Anticipated severe neutropenia lasting more than 7 days is a high-risk criterion.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Alemtuzumab or CAR-T cell therapy within the past 6 months constitutes a high-risk criterion.

questions.explanation: MASCC <21, CISNE ≥3 (in solid tumour patients), and anticipated neutropenia >7 days are all validated high-risk criteria requiring IV empiric therapy. Statement D is false: the guideline specifies alemtuzumab or CAR-T cell therapy within the past 2 months — not 6 months. This is a clinically important distinction because the profound T-cell depletion induced by these agents wanes significantly but is most dangerous in the first weeks to months after administration.
questions.topic: febrile neutropenia MASCC CISNE high-risk alemtuzumab CAR-T 2 months not 6 months trap

questions.question: Regarding empiric antibiotic therapy in high-risk febrile neutropenia:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or carbapenem) is the recommended initial empiric regimen.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Vancomycin should be included routinely in the initial empiric regimen for all high-risk patients.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Aminoglycoside monotherapy is not recommended as the sole initial empirical treatment.

statements.label: D
statements.text: For suspected intra-abdominal infection, anaerobic coverage must be added to the regimen.

questions.explanation: Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy is the cornerstone of high-risk febrile neutropenia management. Statement B is false: vancomycin is not a routine addition — it is reserved for specific indications (haemodynamic instability, MRSA colonisation, gram-positive bacteraemia, catheter or skin/soft tissue infection). Routine vancomycin use increases toxicity without improving outcomes in unselected patients. Aminoglycoside monotherapy is inferior and nephrotoxic. Anaerobic cover (e.g., metronidazole or a carbapenem) is essential whenever intra-abdominal infection is suspected.
questions.topic: empiric antibiotics febrile neutropenia beta-lactam monotherapy vancomycin not routine aminoglycoside anaerobic

questions.question: Regarding fungal pathogens and antifungal management in neutropenic patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Fungi rarely cause the first febrile episode; they typically appear as a cause of persistent or recurrent neutropenic fever.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Candida spp. primarily infect neutropenic patients through inhalation of airborne spores.

statements.label: C
statements.text: In patients receiving fluconazole prophylaxis, breakthrough invasive infections are typically due to fluconazole-resistant Candida (C. glabrata, C. krusei) or Aspergillus spp.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Routine G-CSF use is recommended for neutropenic patients with established febrile episodes.

questions.explanation: Fungi emerge as late complications of prolonged neutropenia and rarely cause the initial febrile episode. Statement B is false: Candida spp. infect through gastrointestinal colonisation and translocation — inhalation of spores is the route for Aspergillus spp. Statement C correctly identifies the shift in breakthrough fungal pathogens when azole prophylaxis is used. Routine G-CSF for established febrile neutropenia is controversial and is not recommended by current guidelines; its prophylactic use before chemotherapy is a distinct indication.
questions.topic: Candida GI translocation Aspergillus inhalation fluconazole breakthrough C glabrata C krusei G-CSF not routine

questions.question: Regarding the timeline of infections following solid organ transplantation (SOT):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Infections in the first month post-SOT are predominantly donor-derived or related to surgical complications.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The greatest risk for opportunistic infections (CMV, Pneumocystis jirovecii) occurs during the 1–6 month post-transplant period.

statements.label: C
statements.text: After 6 months, all solid organ transplant recipients return to the same infection risk as the general population.

statements.label: D
statements.text: HSV reactivation in the first month post-transplant is largely prevented by prophylactic aciclovir.

questions.explanation: The three-period framework underpins SOT infection management. Statement C is false: patients who develop multiple episodes of acute rejection or chronic rejection requiring intensified immunosuppression remain at elevated opportunistic infection risk beyond 6 months and may require prolonged antimicrobial prophylaxis. Only those on stable, reduced immunosuppression approach community infection rates. Prophylactic aciclovir is highly effective at preventing first-month HSV reactivation.
questions.topic: SOT infection 3 periods first month surgical donor 1-6 months CMV PCP chronic rejection ongoing risk

questions.question: Regarding cytomegalovirus (CMV) in solid organ transplant recipients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The donor-positive/recipient-negative (D+/R−) combination carries the highest risk for CMV infection after SOT.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Ganciclovir and valganciclovir prophylaxis frequently causes myelosuppression and neutropenia as a side effect.

statements.label: C
statements.text: CMV serology (IgM/IgG antibody titres) is reliable for diagnosing active CMV disease in transplant recipients.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Through its immunomodulatory effects, CMV infection can precipitate graft rejection and predispose to secondary opportunistic infections.

questions.explanation: D+/R− carries the highest CMV risk as the seronegative recipient has no prior immunity. Myelosuppression — including neutropenia — is a well-recognised and clinically significant side effect of ganciclovir/valganciclovir; this can necessitate switching to a pre-emptive monitoring strategy. Statement C is false: under immunosuppression, antibody responses are unreliable markers of active viraemia; CMV serology is used pre-transplant for risk stratification only. CMV's immunomodulatory effects genuinely increase the risk of graft rejection and secondary infections — it is not merely coincidental.
questions.topic: CMV D+R- highest risk ganciclovir myelosuppression serology not active disease graft rejection immunomodulation

questions.question: Regarding the infection timeline after allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT):

statements.label: A
statements.text: The pre-engraftment phase (day 0–30) is primarily characterised by bacterial infections and invasive Candida.

statements.label: B
statements.text: CMV reactivation is the predominant viral complication during the pre-engraftment phase.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Patients with chronic graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) beyond day 100 remain at sustained high risk of infection due to ongoing immunosuppression.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In the late post-engraftment phase, functional asplenia predisposes to infections with encapsulated organisms such as Streptococcus pneumoniae.

questions.explanation: The pre-engraftment phase is dominated by bacterial infections (gram-positive and gram-negative) and Candida from gut translocation; HSV reactivation also occurs. Statement B is false: CMV reactivation occurs primarily in the early post-engraftment phase (day 30–100), after immune reconstitution has partially begun and cell-mediated immunity is insufficient to control latent virus. Chronic GVHD beyond day 100 perpetuates infection risk through necessary immunosuppressive therapy. Functional asplenia in HSCT creates susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria.
questions.section: Infection following haematopoietic stem cell transplantation
questions.topic: HSCT timeline pre-engraftment bacteria Candida HSV CMV post-engraftment chronic GVHD encapsulated asplenia

questions.question: Regarding adenovirus infection in haematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Adenovirus infection in HSCT is most commonly caused by reactivation from the gastrointestinal tract.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Disseminated adenovirus disease in HSCT carries a reported mortality of 8–26%.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Cidofovir is the most widely used antiviral agent for adenovirus infection in HSCT patients.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Adenovirus infection is more common after autologous than allogeneic HSCT.

questions.explanation: Adenovirus reactivates from the gastrointestinal tract and is predominantly a complication of allogeneic — not autologous — HSCT. Risk is highest with T-cell depletion, myeloablative conditioning, severe GVHD, and mismatched unrelated donor grafts, because adenovirus-specific T-cell surveillance is critically impaired. Statement D is therefore false. The high mortality of disseminated disease and the absence of highly effective antivirals make cidofovir (despite nephrotoxicity) the standard treatment, alongside reduction of immunosuppression where feasible.
questions.section: Infection following haematopoietic stem cell transplantation
questions.topic: adenovirus HSCT GI reactivation cidofovir 8-26% mortality allogeneic T-cell depletion autologous not common

questions.question: Regarding HIV/AIDS patients admitted to the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: PCP is the most common AIDS-defining cause of ICU admission.

statements.label: B
statements.text: PCP in HIV-infected patients typically presents as acute respiratory failure developing over 24–48 hours.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Adjunctive corticosteroids should be added to TMP-SMX when PaO2 on room air is <70 mmHg.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In-hospital mortality of HIV-infected patients in the ICU is primarily determined by CD4 count and HIV viral load.

questions.explanation: PCP remains the leading AIDS-defining diagnosis precipitating ICU admission, particularly in patients with previously undiagnosed HIV. Statement B is false: PCP in HIV-infected patients has a characteristically gradual onset over 2–4 weeks — in contrast to the more fulminant presentation seen in organ transplant or haematological patients. Adjunctive prednisone significantly reduces respiratory failure and mortality when PaO2 <70 mmHg on room air by attenuating the inflammatory response. Statement D is false: ICU mortality of HIV patients is determined by the severity of organ dysfunction and comorbidities, not by HIV-specific parameters (CD4, viral load, prior cART use).
questions.section: The ICU patient with HIV disease
questions.topic: HIV PCP most common AIDS ICU gradual onset 2-4 weeks prednisone PaO2 70 mortality organ dysfunction not CD4

questions.question: Regarding HBV and HCV management in the context of liver transplantation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The main risk factor for HBV reinfection of the transplanted liver graft is detectable HBV DNA at the time of transplantation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Entecavir or tenofovir should be initiated before liver transplantation to suppress HBV replication.

statements.label: C
statements.text: According to EASL, patients with HCV-related decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C) and MELD >18–20 should be treated with DAAs before liver transplantation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Without antiviral therapy, HCV reinfection of the transplanted liver graft is universal.

questions.explanation: Detectable HBV DNA at transplantation is the primary driver of graft reinfection, rapid progressive liver disease and graft loss; suppression with entecavir or tenofovir pretransplant is standard. Statement C is false — it inverts the EASL criterion: patients with MELD <18–20 have adequate hepatic reserve to tolerate and benefit from DAA pretransplant; those with MELD >18–20 should be transplanted first (hepatic function is too poor to wait for treatment) and treated post-transplant if the anticipated wait is <6 months. Universal HCV graft reinfection without antivirals has historically been the rule, and this is why DAA therapy before or after transplantation has transformed outcomes.
questions.topic: HBV DNA reinfection entecavir tenofovir EASL HCV MELD <18-20 pretransplant >18-20 transplant first
